definition: A class or module should have one, and only one, reason to change.
ruleOfThumb: Ask 'who are the different people who might ask me to change this class?' If there's more than one answer, split it.

citation.author: Robert C. Martin
citation.bookTitle: Clean Code: A Handbook of Agile Software Craftsmanship

questions.prompt: This class will need to change for multiple unrelated reasons. What's the clearest sign?

options.text: Parsing, validation, database writes, email delivery, and logging all live in one method — a change to any of them forces this class to change
options.explanation: Each of those five concerns has a different owner: the data team changes validation rules, the DBA changes the schema, the marketing team changes email copy, the platform team changes logging. Every stakeholder causes this one class to change.

options.text: JSON.parse on untrusted input is unsafe without error handling
options.explanation: That's a valid security concern, but it's separate from the structural issue. Even with safe parsing, a class that combines validation, persistence, and notification still has multiple reasons to change.

options.text: The method is too long and should be broken into smaller private methods
options.explanation: Extract Method would help readability, but private helpers inside the same class don't remove the coupling — the class still changes whenever validation rules, the DB schema, or email templates change.

options.text: db and sendEmail should be injected as dependencies rather than called directly
options.explanation: Dependency injection is a good practice for testability, but it doesn't solve the structural problem. Even with injected deps, this class is still responsible for five separate concerns.

questions.prompt: The team needs to replace the database ORM and switch email providers at the same time. What does this design mean for the code change?

options.text: Both unrelated changes land in this one class — database concerns and notification concerns are coupled here, so changing one risks introducing bugs in the other
options.explanation: The ORM swap and the email provider switch have nothing to do with each other — they come from different teams for different reasons. But because OrderProcessor owns both, the same file has to absorb both changes simultaneously.

options.text: Both changes are safe because they're in a single, well-contained method
options.explanation: Being in one method doesn't make changes safe — it means unrelated changes are coupled. A mistake in the email block can break the database logic, even though they have nothing to do with each other.

options.text: The class is fine; the real fix is injecting db and sendgrid as constructor dependencies
options.explanation: DI improves testability but doesn't separate responsibilities. With injected deps, this class still changes for both database reasons and notification reasons — different stakeholders, one class.

options.text: The method needs a try/catch block to ensure the email always sends even if the DB fails
options.explanation: Error handling is a separate concern. The structural problem — database and notification logic living together — exists whether or not there's a try/catch.

questions.prompt: The team needs to upgrade the PDF library and switch email providers — two separate changes. Which version requires touching the fewest separate classes?

options.text: Version A — both unrelated changes modify the same class. A PDF library upgrade and an email provider switch land in ReportService even though they have nothing to do with each other.
options.explanation: In Version A, one class absorbs both changes — that's two separate reasons to change. In Version B, the PDF upgrade touches only PDFRenderer and the email switch touches only EmailDelivery. Each change is contained, with no risk of cross-contamination.

options.text: Version B — three separate classes means the changes are spread across more files, which is harder to coordinate
options.explanation: Touching more files is not inherently harder — it's safer. Each file has a clear scope, so the developer changing PDFRenderer doesn't need to understand email delivery. Coordination complexity comes from coupled responsibilities, not separated ones.
